claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period

Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to gather some information about your situation.

Let me start with a few key questions:

**How many children are you looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm looking to claim Child Benefit for my two children. I have Alex, who is 17, and Blake, who's 18.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have two children: Alex who is 17, and Blake who is 18.

Since both of your children are 16 or over, the eligibility rules are a bit different. Let me ask you about their current situation:

**Are Alex and Blake currently in approved education or training (for example, at school, college, or on an apprenticeship)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is still in college doing A-levels, but Blake has finished with education - they're not studying anymore.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. So Alex is in approved education (A-levels at college), which is helpful for their eligibility.

For Blake, who has finished with education and is no longer studying, I need to understand their current circumstances:

**Is Blake currently in paid work, and if so, how many hours per week are they working?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't working at the moment. They've just been at home since finishing their studies.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. That's helpful to know.

Now, since Blake has left education and isn't working, I need to check one more thing about their situation:

**Has Blake registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services since leaving education?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake hasn't registered with any careers service or anything like that. They've just been at home really.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now let me check on Alex's situation in a bit more detail:

**Is Alex still under 20 years old, and are they expected to continue in their A-level studies?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is 17, so definitely under 20, and yes, they're continuing with their A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just to confirm the full picture of your family situation:

**Do you live in the UK, and are you responsible for both Alex and Blake (living with them and/or contributing to their upkeep)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. Both Alex and Blake live at home with me, and I'm responsible for them both.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you for that confirmation. I now have all the information I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both children.

Let me explain the outcome:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary

**You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex, but not for Blake.**

**For Alex (17, in A-levels):** ✅ **Eligible**
- Alex is under 20 and remains in approved education (A-levels), so you qualify for Child Benefit for them.

**For Blake (18, not in education):** ❌ **Not Eligible**
- Blake is 18 and is no longer in approved education or training. They haven't registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, and the special 20-week provision only applies to 16 or 17 year-olds who leave education. Therefore, they don't qualify for Child Benefit.

You can proceed to claim Child Benefit for Alex. Remember that only one person can claim per child, so if anyone else is also claiming for Alex, you would need to agree who should receive it, or HMRC will decide.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and under 20. They are currently in approved education (A-levels at college) and continue to study. According to the rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16, and for children 16 or over, you'll usually still qualify if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex meets all these criteria - living with you in the UK, under 20, and in approved education - they are eligible for Child Benefit.

Blake is 18 years old and has finished with education. They are not currently in paid work, nor have they registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services since leaving education. According to the rules, if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if they register with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Since Blake is 18 (not 16 or 17) and has neither registered with a careers service nor joined the armed services, and they are not in approved education or training, they do not qualify for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex, who is 17 and in approved education (A-levels). However, you are not eligible for Blake, who is 18 and no longer in approved education or training, nor registered with a careers service or the armed services. Only one person can claim per child, and your eligibility is determined per child based on their individual circumstances."

According to the rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16, and for children 16 or over, you'll usually still qualify if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex meets all these criteria - living with you in the UK, under 20, and in approved education - they are eligible for Child Benefit.

Blake is 18 years old and has finished with education. They are not currently in paid work, nor have they registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services since leaving education. According to the rules, if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if they register with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Since Blake is 18 (not 16 or 17) and has neither registered with a careers service nor joined the armed services, and they are not in approved education or training, they do not qualify for Child Benefit.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex, who is 17 and in approved education (A-levels). However, you are not eligible for Blake, who is 18 and no longer in approved education or training, nor registered with a careers service or the armed services. Only one person can claim per child, and your eligibility is determined per child based on their individual circumstances."
